speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Soon, the rumors were that the-- that everybody was going to be deported back to Germany. At that point, the committee came to the parents of the younger children and said that they can take everyone under 18 out of the camp and get them into homes, children's homes. And I absolutely refused to do anything of the sort. I wanted to go with my mother and father. By this time, my parents had been permitted to sleep in the same area. The mothers-- the women and men started moving in together and I was there, too. And the discussion was frequent and constant. And Mrs. Solomon, who had been a good friend, who was the director of OSE in southern France that I had known for many years, came to see us. And I had been in a summer camp called Chateau [NON-ENGLISH] in the summer of 1941 for a month and liked it very much. And she said to me, I don't want you to go with your parents. I want you to stay with us and I will take you back to [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was a promise that she made to me and which she kept. So a decision was made that my things would be separated from everybody else's and I would leave. My parents were very sad-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and yet they were upbeat towards me. But you can feel it. On the 10th of August, buses rolled into the camp and it was time for us to leave. We assembled by the buses and there was a guard at each bus. I still see him sitting there-- standing. And my father-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --put his hand on my head and gave me the priestly blessing-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --which he would have done every holiday and every Shabbat. And I knew this was that last time that it would ever happen. And as he and other fathers did that, the guard walked away, turned around, and cried just like I'm crying. The reason I'm mentioning it is that as the roll was called, there were several children that were sick and the committee had people standing by. And every time a name wasn't answered, someone else jumped into the bus. And so we had extra people in the bus. The guard didn't pay attention. And we got out of the camp, the bus was loaded, two buses were loaded, and we were taken out of the camp. We were maybe a mile away from camp when the buses suddenly stopped, the doors opened up, and there were others-- youngsters, two in each bus-- that came in. They had been tied to the bottom of the buses in camp and had ridden out on the buses to escape. These were 18-, 19-year-olds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came into the buses and then we went on to Marseilles. We were taken to Hotel Bonaparte, which is another one of the internment hotels in Marseille. And we were taken there and left there. The ones that had been hidden in the buses were left off in the city and were met by underground people and they left immediately from there. In Hotel Bonaparte, we were with some older people who had been left behind when all of the other women and children were taken to Les Milles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a cook who was also a resistance fighter who was our contact. He saw to everything. A few weeks after we were there, word came down that everybody who was under 16 had to leave. So he hid a bunch of them in the coal bins in the basement and in the water tanks on the roof. These all had cisterns on the roof to supply water for the building. And they stayed there for about two or three days while the Germans were looking for them. After the heat was off, he smuggled these people out. And later on his milk cart that he took out every morning to get milk and groceries for the kitchen, he would take their suitcases out gradually so that their belongings would disappear, too. This happened twice, once for everybody up to 16, then once for everybody above 14. I was 13 at the time, so I was still relatively lucky. There was a diphtheria scare, I spent some time in the hospital getting shots, and then came back to the hotel. And finally in October, we were sent to the children's homes. We were put on trains and taken to Toulouse. In Toulouse, we were split up into the five camps in the area. And I was with several others taken to Chateau [NON-ENGLISH], which is near [NON-ENGLISH] in the [NON-ENGLISH] province of France. I have been back there since. And in fact, it was also the camp-- the children's home-- where Klaus [NON-ENGLISH] was when he was a little boy and was separated from his father. I found this out just recently when I saw-- when I found his book at the Holocaust Museum in Washington. This was a place where I was exceedingly happy. It was organized, it was ordered, it was clean. We had food, we were schooled, and we were free to do what we wanted as children. We had work responsibilities, they had a full farm there raising rabbits, chickens, and pigs which were used for food. They had a field where they grew their own potatoes and many other things. About three weeks later, word came that the American embassy wanted us back in Marseille in order to process our papers for our children's transport. We got back on the trains, back to Marseilles, to another hotel, which was filthy. I'll never forget. It inoculated me for any dirty food that I will ever see because one morning I was responsible for making the coffee and they told me the ground coffee is down in the basement in bags. I picked up this burlap sack which had ground coffee in it. It was crawling with ants. And I went back up and I said to the cook, there's ants in the coffee. She says, they won't eat anything. Make the coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made the coffee with the ants. And that to me was the worst that I've ever seen. It was dirty, it was filthy. When we got the Marseilles that first morning, the Allies landed in North Africa. And we were told that the American ambassador had left, war was declared with America, now there would be no more transport. So after about five, six days in Marseille, we were sent back to our camps and I went back to [NON-ENGLISH] very happily.

speaker: interviewee
text: In January, a measles epidemic hit the children's camp and I was infected and was in bed for three weeks. I got back up and in late January, early February a mumps epidemic hit and I got the mumps. I was very, very sick. And about usually you figure three weeks in bed with the mumps. Having had a relapse and high fever, I figured I would still be there for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, the doctor came in. He checked my pulse, checked my temperature. He says, go get dressed. What is this? Just go get dressed and then go see the director. I didn't know what was going on. I went to my room, got dressed, went down to the director's office, Mrs. Bloch, and she said, you and one other boy-- a boy by the name of Hans Kirchheimer-- were supposed to leave that day for Switzerland. He says, you have to get out of here. Go pack. So I packed and around five o'clock in the afternoon, just dusk, a bus-- a little mini bus pulled up and picked us up and took us to Toulouse. There were other children in the bus plus a lady. And when we got on the bus, she handed us papers with names and she said, memorize these. I was now Gerard. My last name I cannot remember. These were false passports and they gave us to travel with in France.

speaker: interviewee
text: At one point, they told us to tell people that we were-- were-- had been sick and we were going to a sanatorium near [NON-ENGLISH] in the high Alps. Later on, they told us to tell us-- tell people if we were asked that we were on a school trip the lady traveled with us from Toulouse on the train all the way to [NON-ENGLISH]. In [NON-ENGLISH] we got off the train and went to the Hotel de la Garde, directly across the street. We got something to eat and then they took all of us-- there was 12 of us-- into a room upstairs. And they told us that we would be taken to Switzerland sometime within the next 24, hours just to rest. We had been on the train, it had been exciting. And naturally, in that kind of situation, you can't really sleep but we rested. Around four o'clock she came back in, she divided us into two groups-- eight in one group, four in another. The eight were-- I was one of the older ones, 13- 14-year-olds down. And we were supposed to go during the daytime. The four older ones were joining an adult group that was going to be going at night through the mountains, a much more rigorous route. Around 4:30, just before it got-- started to get dark, she took us with our luggage. Our luggage was to stay behind, was coming later supposedly. And we walked towards the cemetery area. She had told us that what we would do, we would get to a big field and at the end of the field was a cemetery. We were going to pick dandelion, which is a salad delicacy in Europe in the spring, and put it in brown paper bags. We each had our own brown paper bag. And then by her signal, which was going to be that she disappeared behind the wall of the cemetery, we are to make a beat for the border. The border was on our right. It was a barbed wire double set about six feet high, crisscrossed. Beyond it was a river. We had to go through the river and then up an embankment that's about six foot tall-- remember, we're all little-- and then had to climb up that embankment to get to the other side. The-- the Italian guards that were guarding this border were going to be going to the guardhouse for change of shifts with their dogs. When we saw them going away from the border, we are to go closer to the border, inching closer, keeping an eye on the lady. And then when she disappeared, we were going to make a dash. Meanwhile, we had to look for a hole in the fence to get through. We-- I had a nine-year-old as my responsibility. We all had a younger one to take with us. So we found a spot and said, the barbed wire is fairly loose there, let's try it. So when the signal came, we made a dash for it. We couldn't see any Swiss guards anywhere. And we got through the barbed wire a little at a time, getting hung up, tearing our clothes, going a little further. Then we went into this little river, which in the summer when I saw it again was nothing, but in the spring when the water is melting, it was up to our knees. We get through the water and here we are up against this high wall, which is higher than I am, that could reach at that time. And all of a sudden, arms came down, grabbing us. It was the Swiss guards that had been lying on their stomachs waiting for us. And they grabbed us one at a time, pulled us up out of the water and onto land. And then they stood up with their arms drawn because by then, the Italians heard what was going on, the dogs were raising Cain. And they-- the Swiss said to the Italians, don't do anything, they're on Swiss soil. We were safe. We were taken to a guard shack, where a woman made us some hot chocolate and dried our clothes. Then the bus came and picked us up and we were taken to an assembly point in Geneva, which was a school. And there we were interviewed. And we had been told before we left France that if anybody asked, we were to say there was a man with glasses and a beard who brought us and that we did not know his name because no one should know that it was a woman because they knew information went back and forth between the Swiss and the Germans. Also, we were told we are not to say anything about having any relatives, friends, anybody in France because if they knew we had somebody in France, they would send us back. And so we were interviewed two or three times over the next two or three days. Then after about a week, we would put on a train, some of us, and taken to a home for children in Ascona, Switzerland, which was run by a lady by the name of Lily [NON-ENGLISH]. She was a child psychologist who when the unrest started opened this home for children in Ascona. And there were probably about 40 of us there. Then I found out there was another home that she had another 20. And she take-- took care of us there. We had school. We learned-- this was in the Italian part of Switzerland-- I had to learn German again because I'd forgotten my German learning French. So we were being taught in French, essentially, but we were also being told, refresh your other languages. While we were there, people would come and interview us. And one of the things that happened was that I mentioned this family [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH] as having been our contact. And I immediately wrote to them, here I am, because I wanted to know where my cousin, Lizel, was, who also was supposed to contact them. Well, she was still in France. But when the committee found out that I had these people there, they suggested I move in with them. Well, they are older people, they have grandchildren. They were in no shape to take care of this 14-year-old or 13-year-old. So they found a Mrs.-- another Mrs. Bloch, who was in charge of the committee, in Winterthur. And this lady found a family who had a two and a half year old little boy who was willing to take on an older boy, feeling that I would be a help rather than a hindrance. And on a trial basis I would put-- was put with these people for a month. Their name was Jacob-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --and Helen Neff. They lived on [NON-ENGLISH] 49 in Winterthur, near Zurich. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They became and still are my second family. They're still alive. I'm in contact with them always. We-- I lived with them for a month, and unknownst to me until 1979, when we went to visit them, did I find out that after months, they said yes, they would keep me indefinitely and they did not want any money for it. They were supposed to be paid. They said, we don't want the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with them. I went to sixth grade secondary-- primary school there, learned Swiss-German, became fluent in it, then went to sixth-- to seventh and eighth grade there through the war. I was part of the family. If they went on vacation, I went on vacation; if they went on a trip, I went on a trip. They were very good parents. They insisted from the moment I got there that I didn't call them Mother and Father but Aunt and Uncle. And they insisted that I continue my Jewish education.

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent me to Hebrew school every Wednesday afternoon when we had free from regular school. And on holidays, I was expected to go to services. They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --never tried to do anything to dissuade me from either my religion or my knowledge of my parents. They honored them and still do. And I was very, very happy there. It was probably only the second time since 1938 that I felt free. The other time was in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to your real parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much later. In 1979, when we went to Israel, I went to Yad Vashem and I inquired whether they had any information. And the gentleman said-- we went-- the first time we went with a tour and there was-- it was closed. So I went back on Sunday morning with Andy. Suzy was packing because we were leaving that night. And Steve was sick from the trots he got in Israel from the water, so he didn't go. We went into the archives and I told this gentleman what I was looking for. And he says, I have just the thing for you. He brought out a book that was written or compiled, rather, by Klaus [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a-- it was a copy of all the teletypes that were sent from Paris to Berlin indicating the transports and who was on them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, here, look through this book. Do you have an idea when? I said, well, they left Le Milles on the 13th of August, probably about the 17th-- which was my birthday, by the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we opened up the book, there were pages-- each train contained 1,000 passengers. And every name and birth date and home town of these people were listed. On the 17th was the day my parents left for Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was in black and white on those sheets. And I looked further and I found my Aunt Ruth and Uncle Ernst left there in September and so did my Uncle Henry, Aunt Elsa, and cousin Martin. He was not exempt because he was over 16. And the destination of all of them was Auschwitz. I never heard from my parents again. I did get a postcard while we were in-- while I was in Switzerland for my Uncle Ernst, which I have here, which told me that he was separated from his wife and that he was working in Silesia, southern Poland. And it was all written in the third person and that he would write again. He was writing as he was his friend and that he would write again. But we never heard anymore from him. He was the youngest of the men, in the best physical shape, and he apparently lasted the longest. I stayed in Switzerland, to get back to that, until after the war. After war-- the war, the Swiss notified me that since I had relatives in other countries, I had a year to emigrate. And I contacted my mother's two sisters in America, my Aunt Nellie, who lived in Philadelphia, who-- and my Aunt Katie, who also lived in Philadelphia. And they and their husbands got together and got me papers and then brought me over here in-- I came over in April of 1946. I got here on the 9th of April. I spent some time in England on the way waiting for a plane. My cousin, Lizel, who had gotten to Switzerland and worked in a factory, I had seen several times. The last time I saw her was just before I left Switzerland. She was very, very sick with an infection and they didn't have penicillin yet and she died from her infection shortly after I came to the States, the 9h of April. And so we lost her also. In Philadelphia, I started going to high school, started 10th grade. I got credit for most of my work in Switzerland and then switched from a-- an academic high school to a vocational high school and learned refrigeration and air conditioning there, which I never took advantage of because the unions were very difficult and you couldn't. I finished high school went to work for an electrician for a while, but in between right after I got there, I started working as a busboy in a restaurant and that was always my first love. I wanted to become a cook. But when I worked in this restaurant, I realized how hot and miserable to work as a cook is in the kitchen and I decided that was not for me. I decided to change to restaurant management as a goal. In 1950, I was working in the Catskills as a waiter when the war broke out with Korea. And knowing my luck, I would be sent in the infantry to Korea. I didn't want that-- enough mud and dirt-- so I enlisted in the Air Force on the 27th of December, 1950 and became a radio man. I spent some time in California, I spent some time in Washington state, and then was shipped to Korea in October, 1951-- 52, after going through radio school at Keesler Air Force Base. And spent a year in Korea, first in Taegu and then in Seoul in the communications centers there. After I came back, I was stationed in-- near Detroit, Willow Run Airport, for just under a year and I was discharged early in order to start attending college. I got an admitted to Paul Smith's College in upstate New York, near Saranac Lake, to study in hotel and restaurant management. It's a junior college, where I spent the first two years. After that, I transferred to Denver University and finished the last two years and had a bachelor's in science in hotel and restaurant management.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here and go to the next tape.
